title: Burner Email Account with Accelerating High-Value Purchase Pattern
narrative: Fraudster creates free webmail account (gmail.com, yahoo.com, outlook.com) with minimal setup and no secondary verification, then immediately initiates purchase attempts. Pattern shows email account age under 24 hours paired with R_emaildomain matching free provider but P_emaildomain null or different provider. Transaction sequence begins with sub-$50 test charges (avg $23-$35) to validate card, followed within 90-180 minutes by escalating purchases: $180-$320 range, then $450-$680. Card velocity shows card1_txn_count_1day jumping from 0 to 4-7 transactions. No historical link between card BIN and this email domain in prior 180 days.Addr1 frequently null or generic (00000, 99999). ProductCD switches from physical goods (W, C) to high-liquidity items (H, S) as amounts increase. CVV match rate 100% initially, suggesting fresh stolen card data rather than card-not-present testing.
indicators: P_emaildomain is free provider (gmail/yahoo/outlook) with R_emaildomain null or mismatched
card1_txn_count_1day increases from 0 to 4+ within 3-hour window
TransactionAmt escalates: first txn $20-$50, subsequent txns $180-$680 within 180 minutes
addr1 is null, 00000, or 99999 in 60%+ of transactions for this email
No historical transactions linking card1 BIN to this email domain in prior 180 days
ProductCD shifts from W/C to H/S as TransactionAmt increases
CVV2 match on all transactions with card1_amt_zscore exceeding 2.8 by fourth transaction
summary: A brand-new free email account (gmail, yahoo, outlook) under 24 hours old starts with small test purchases ($20-50) then rapidly escalates to high-value transactions ($450-680) within 3 hours, using a card that has never been linked to this email domain before. The address is missing or fake (00000, 99999), and purchases shift from physical goods to easily-resold items as amounts climb.
reasoning: The fraudster is using a disposable email to validate stolen card details with small charges, then maximizing the theft window before the card is blocked by making increasingly large purchases of high-liquidity items they can quickly resell. The brand-new email, rapid escalation pattern, fake address, and zero prior relationship between card and email all indicate this is a criminal using fresh stolen payment data rather than a legitimate new customer.
indicator_explanations: Email uses free service but recipient domain is missing or doesn't match
Card activity spikes from zero to multiple transactions within three hours
Purchase amounts ramp up from small test to large fraud hits rapidly
Address fields are mostly missing or placeholder values for this email account
This card's bank has never processed transactions for this email domain before
Merchant categories change from low-risk to high-value goods as amounts climb
All transactions pass CVV check yet spending exceeds typical cardholder baseline significantly
